mappings.rationaleAI: Traces the full history of genetics from Mendel's pea experiments establishing heredity laws through the Modern Synthesis linking genetics to Darwinian evolution, the discovery of DNA structure by Crick and Watson, and developments in genetic manipulation. Spans early speculations (A.1), Mendelian genetics (A.2), molecular genetics and DNA (B.2), and natural selection via the Modern Synthesis (C.2).

Examines the molecular mechanisms of DNA mutation as errors in replication, their role in generating heritable variation for natural selection, and Haldane's contributions to population genetics. Directly addresses the genetic code and its mutations (B.2), population-level gene frequency changes (C.1), and natural selection acting on mutational variation (C.2).

Traces the intellectual lineage from Mendel's heredity laws through DNA discovery to debates about genes governing human traits, directly engaging the nature-versus-nurture question. Addresses Mendelian genetics (A.2), the combined action of heredity and environment on organism traits (A.3), and the molecular basis of hereditary claims via DNA (B.2).

Examines what occurs when organisms with different genetic constitutions interbreed, producing sterile or fertile offspring with novel trait combinations through hybrid vigor. Darwin's study of domestication and artificial selection in agriculture and animal breeding connects to C.3, while the genetic mechanisms of speciation through hybridization involve chromosomal processes (B.1) and natural selection (C.2).

Lamarck's theory of inheritance of acquired characteristics represents a key early speculation on heredity (A.1), and Darwin's effort to distinguish natural selection from Lamarckian inheritance frames the debate that Mendelian genetics ultimately resolved (A.2). Discussion of epigenetics connects historical heredity debates to modern molecular understanding of inheritance.

Lysenko's state-enforced rejection of Mendelian genetics in favour of Lamarckian inheritance of acquired characteristics devastated Soviet biological sciences for decades. Directly engages competing early theories of inheritance (A.1), the suppression and vindication of Mendel's laws (A.2), and the ideological rejection of Darwinian natural selection (C.2).

The cell as the fundamental structural unit of life underpins the cellular basis of heredity, with cell division transmitting genetic material across generations. The episode explicitly notes that cell biology underpins genetics and developmental biology, connecting directly to chromosomes and genes at the cellular level (B.1).

DNA is highlighted as an extraordinarily complex informational polymer, and protein folding is covered as a key macromolecular process. Directly relevant to the chemical and molecular nature of genes (B.2), explaining the polymer chemistry underlying genetic information storage and protein expression.

Mitochondria possess their own circular DNA inherited maternally, providing a distinct non-nuclear mode of genetic transmission. Their role in tracing human evolutionary lineage through mitochondrial DNA connects the cellular basis of heredity (B.1) with the molecular nature of genes and applications of molecular genetics in population studies (B.2).

The eugenics movement promoting selective breeding of humans represents the most controversial historical application of hereditary principles to deliberate genetic modification of human populations (C.3). Illustrates how concepts of heredity and selection were appropriated for programs of artificial selection applied to humankind.
